FROM: Nathan Terranova RA, NCARB, WELL AP
CONTENT: Hey man! Yeah we use a ton of AI tools. I tried Cursor a bit and wanted to try Windsurf next just haven't gotten to it yet. I have switched to use JetBrains Rider as my primary IDE. In there I use their built in AI tooling and GitHub Copilot (both also have agentic editing capabilities) but actually the most impressive coding tool I've used so far is Codex from OpenAI. It's run through ChatGPT, but basically we hooked it up to GitHub and can point it to a specific branch on a specific repo and have it go perform multiple tasks or investigate issues. It can build the solution and run tests, although we're still troubleshooting that part, but it's gonna get super powerful. Basically we can take the bugs being reported by users and have it go investigate and propose solutions. It can create a new branch with its changes and setup a Pull Request, so we essentially pull that branch locally and test it. If we like the fix we just merge the PR

Geoff, hope all is well in the new year! I am at a new company, Corgan, and am focusing on Architectural R&D, and it’s been pretty amazing getting back into the academic mindset and how all of our new age tech can be leveraged to build better environments. Every month we have a session called 'Design Advocates’ and it is a group within Corgan that develops strategies for implementing innovative ideas into our workflow and practice. Each month, the group convenes to hear from experts in design focused and design-adjacent fields and yours and Christines Wacta’s names popped right into my head! This month, we want to expand our scope and learn about how geospatial analytics and geospatial data impact urban design and architecture as geospatial data is an increasingly becoming a relevant topic for our practice as we think more about the impact of design at greater scopes.  This discussion is intended to provide an overview of how geospatial data and geospatial analytics/tools can impact design and urban planning and how we can leverage them for gaining unique insights into the built and future environment. It would be over teams and the audience would consist of designers/architects across Corgan’s market sectors and is intended to be around 45 minutes with a brief Q/A at the end. I’ve also reached out to Christine Wacta as well and we would really enjoy both of yells perspective on this! It would be Monday, March 11, Noon - 1:30. Let me know if your interested in sharing some insight!

Hi Alexander Excited to share our October newsletter! This month, we’re highlighting some of the MEP design projects we’ve recently started. Check it out and let us know what you think!  TURTLE CREEK WINERY GDI Engineering is tasked with the MEP design of the TURTLE CREEK Winery facility. The MEP set for the winery includes a mechanical system that considers air conditioning and ventilation split into two systems, an electrical system that includes full lighting design for the building interior, including lighting controls and code compliant values for the lighting power, and a plumbing system that includes water supply and sewer networks for the proposed restrooms, mechanical equipment, and other area drainages Client: Turtle Creek Vineyard Location: 183 Fall Creek Rd Kerville, Texas Area: 10,400 SF Category: COMMERCIAL Duration of the project: 20 working days  FOREST TRAIL COMPLEX GDI Engineering has been assigned the MEP design of the Forest Trail Complex, which comprises of five buildings in several phases. Buildings include residence, brewery, workshop, animal hospital, nursery, and cottage. The mechanical system will include air conditioning and ventilation for the cottage, while other buildings will have ventilation in spaces where required and heating for occupied spaces. Additionally, ceiling HVLS fans will be installed. The electrical design will include full lighting for the building interior, including lighting controls and fixtures. The plumbing design will include water supply and sewer networks for the proposed buildings, mechanical equipment, and other area drainages. Client: Webber + Studio. INC Construction Date: Location: Leander, Texas 78641 Area: 4312 SF for phase 1 Category: Residential / Commercial Duration of the project: 25 working days  FRULLATI CAFÉ GDI Engineering has been assigned the MEP design of Frullati Café. The scope of work includes mechanical layout for space heating and ventilation, schedules of equipment for mechanical and ventilation equipment, power plans, lighting plans, water supply layout, drainage layouts, water and drainage fixtures units/loads calculations, gas layout, and gas isometric layout. Client: Bluestone Partners, LLC Location: San Marcos, Texas 78666 Area: 2001 SF Category: Hospitality Duration of the project: 21 working days  here you can download the newsletter https://gdicwins1.sharepoint.com/:f:/g/EhP8MOYh-LBImFV7LqSN1SMBbyuITCct9JX6T_ZJFn-MCg?e=QL4F1B  Regards,  Antone

SUBJECT: Opportunity at RSA - $7K Signing Bonus
CONTENT: Hello Alexander,  I am looking for young developing architects.  Your profile stands out to me Alexander, so I wanted to reach out and see if you would be interested in visiting about a leadership-track position currently available in our firm.  We are searching for young, vibrant architects who are looking for the right opportunity.  And that is exactly what we offer, opportunity to young architects.  Our offer includes a signing bonus of $7,000 as well as five and a half weeks of paid time off (which breaks down to be 4 weeks of personal PTO and 7-1/2 paid holidays). We believe in a work-life balance at RSA. We offer several other great benefits to our employees that I can explain if we can visit further. Our senior staff is within a couple years of retirement, so we are looking to hire a young architect who can develop into a VP and help usher the firm into the next generation.  I am very interested in visiting with you further about this position, because we have been awarded some fantastic projects recently and are looking to strategically grow our talented staff.  Do you have time for coffee to chat further about RSA and this opportunity?  I look forward to hearing back from you, Alexander.  Best regards,  Randall Scott, AIA Founding Principal Randall Scott Architects, Inc.

Hey! First off, killer capstone project!!! So computer wise, so going in I purchased a HP Envy 4K 17” for $1000 for studio and already had a tower at home which was a 5820k on a 120mm loop, 32gb Ram, with a 8gb 1080 on two 1440 27” benQ design monitors. Would have to say that was only one with a studio/home set up with everyone doing laptop or PC in studio with monitor set ups. The new building is amazing and both have really good security and never heard of a story of somthing going missing. For the printing simulation in LadybugHoneybee Galapagos/Octopus Millipede/Karamba3D I upgraded my cpu starting the thesis work to a 2950x with an expanded loop and upgraded 64gb ram kit where pushing the 3D printing work on my old cpu just wasn’t working. I would say a laptop is mandatory for home/studio and then if can an iPad Pro is a HUGE plus, such a great asset to design on and take to class and definitely worth it. And then if really need the extra horses would get a tower. I personally use a Galaxy S4 for maxing AR apps, but would go with iPad if wasn’t, currently saving for the new Lidar 12.8”! There were some who had a tower at studio, but most used laptop on dual monitors so could take it home or workshops which there were a few etc. For me personally the laptop I have works great at work or when was at studio/workshops but am able to leave the heavy lifting and big designing for at home. If can make a laptop work there are some good ones out there, but have to be real isn’t a tower ya know. Only downside of tower/laptop combo is can get pretty pricy when add everything up. Have you also considered a printer? Would highly recommend one for models and creativity! My cell is 404 285 2521 if you need any help deciding on something!

TO: Amanda Ruth Joab,Christine Wacta,  P.h.D / Humanist Architect
CONTENT: Great school!! Tons of visiting faculty from around the globe, my current studio is being head by Patrick Gmür, the former City Urban Planner for Switzerland and very well known architect (I bet Christine is turning in her chair with that Urban Design talk(plus a dedicated GIS department))!. Have really enjoyed the outside views the visiting faculty bring, opens a lot of new doors and honestly new ways to design. The campus is a legit 12/10... absolutely amazing and sprinkled with places to eat and be at. The city is booming, 8 billion in development last year, and great food! The city itself is very historic as was tied in with Chicago in 1900s so a lot of the same vibes as Savannah which is nice for such a long move! It is a design oriented school but the majority of the Professors are practicing and have done some serious work so each has great insight into applications of projects and can give great design advice! The faculty is so friendly and always willing to help! Great vibes! They recently finished the End Transformation which is 6 new buildings, definitely check that out as Architecture got an entirely new fresh building Weil Hall to spread into! Library is huge and arch has their own mini one which is nice to go into! Printing, (when goes smoothly, think this is a nation wide issue) is cheap, $1.50 a sqft to plot. Last but not least, great respectful reputation! For me, reaaaaly glad I applied and am very proud to be graduating from the 2 year program next year! Definitely a worth accelerated program and it will be a hard City to move away from! Also one of the professors Chandler Ahrens who I had my first semester and hopefully again for my degree project is a SCAD alumni who has done some impressive design work at Morphosis such as Cooper Union Buiding, interesting about the SCAD connect here! I can connect you with Ellen Bailey who is our Graduate Program Cordiantor who can give you more information if you would like! Really is an amazing school to be apart of and feel valued at!
